feedback.text: The objectives of the Digital Fairness Act are largely content on focusing on the digital content, but it should also touch on the issue of fair ownership of digital content. For example, if a person purchases a video or a computer game, the Act should include a goal and a policy defining the continued ownership of this digital good. Currently, the video and computer game industry has a model where they sell games to the general consumer, which the consumer does not own, according to the EULA found in these games. Companies like Ubisoft have a clause in their EULA that demands destruction of purchased digital goods if Ubisoft so demands. This can be seen as part of the difficulties with digital contracts, as companies always redefine the term "purchase" as something akin to a long-term license rental, which they can pull out at any time for any reason. This sort of redefining language is also part of manipulating and deceiving the customer, leading to all customers lacking control over the product they've purchased. There should be a demand for clear-cut language, where there'd be room to redefine commonly used terms and words in this harmful manner. However, the more pressing issue is regarding the destruction of these computer and video games. While the medium is young compared to literature, cinema or music, video games have already eclipsed all its predecessors as the most lucrative and most popular market globally. They represent the modern world's culture and are in danger of being lost to time, much like so many of the classical arts have been lost. If publishers were required to leave their games in a functional, playable state after they end their support on these games, we would further consumer rights to the digital products they've purchased as well as ensure the continuing archival of culture. A European Citizen's Movement "Stop Killing Games" [shortened as SKG] is already addressing this in a fashion, with nearly million and a half signatures under its belt, I see fit that the Digital Fairness Act would also add a section similar to SKG, as numerous games have already been killed as publishers have simply pulled the plug, so to speak, disabling customers' access to the games and digital goods within these games they've purchased. Video and computer games are excluded from digital goods classification by default, as this gives the publishers an unfair advantage over the customer. To use an example, a 2014 video game "The Crew" was delisted from digital storefronts and had all microtransactions suspended in 2023. Following this, the game's servers were shut down in April 2024, which lead its publisher, Ubisfot, revoking all licenses from the consumers without refunds or ways to re-download the game files. However, before this users had found an unused offline mode within the game's programming, which Ubisoft never put into action. In effect, customers lost all access to The Crew game and everything they've purchased via microtransactions. The kind of predatory and unfair business is not uncommon in the computer and video game industry. Effectively speaking, all companies able to enact on these consumer abusing patterns do so as much as possible, and there is no legislation to defend the customer. I believe the Digital Fairness Act would do justice to include a section to its goals, where the publishers are required to leave games they sell in functional, playable state, where the customer is able to play their game as advertised.
